title: Abstraction is pattern extraction
idea: When you notice the same structure in two different situations, and you name that structure -- that's abstraction. It's not about making things vague. It's about **extracting the pattern that two concrete things share**.
example:
```
Sorting a deck of cards
Sorting names alphabetically
Sorting files by date
```
The concrete details differ completely. But the pattern -- *compare two things, swap if out of order, repeat* -- is identical. Naming that pattern "sorting" is abstraction.
probe: If abstraction is pattern extraction, then a *bad* abstraction is one that groups things by surface similarity rather than shared structure. Can you think of a category that feels natural but groups things that actually work differently?

title: Delay causes oscillation
idea: When there's a **delay between action and effect**, systems overshoot. You keep pushing because you haven't seen the result yet. Then the result arrives all at once, so you overcorrect. This creates oscillation -- swinging back and forth.
example: A shower with slow pipes. You turn the hot knob -- nothing happens. Turn more. Still cold. Turn more. Suddenly: scalding. You crank it cold. Wait. Still hot. Crank more. Then: freezing. The delay between your action and the temperature change causes you to oscillate.
probe: Central banks raise interest rates to cool inflation. But rate changes take 12-18 months to fully affect the economy. How does this delay make monetary policy inherently oscillatory? (And what does the Fed do to compensate?)

title: Emergence: the whole surprises
idea: When components interact, the system can exhibit behaviors that **none of the individual components have**. This isn't magic -- it's a consequence of the interaction rules. But you can't predict it by studying parts in isolation.
example: Each neuron in your brain is simple: receive signals, fire or don't. But 86 billion of them interacting produce consciousness, creativity, humor. No single neuron is conscious. Consciousness is an emergent property of their interaction.
probe: A traffic jam can form even when no one slows down first -- just from cars being slightly too close. The jam is "real" (you sit in it) but has no physical substance. It's a pattern in the flow. What other emergent things feel real but have no physical form?

title: Exponential growth: the doubling trick
idea: Anything growing by a **fixed percentage** is growing exponentially. The key intuition: use the **Rule of 72**. Divide 72 by the growth rate to get the doubling time. 6% growth? Doubles in 12 years. 1% growth? 72 years.
example: If a city grows at 3% per year, it doubles in 72 / 3 = 24 years. Sounds slow -- but doubling means the city needs to build as much infrastructure in the *next* 24 years as it built in its *entire history*. That's why exponential growth is hard to manage.
probe: Bacteria double every 20 minutes. Start with one. After 10 hours (30 doublings): 2^30 ~ 1 billion. After 10.5 hours (just 3 more doublings): 8 billion. Why does the last half-hour matter more than the first 9 hours?

title: Feedback loops amplify or stabilize
idea: When the output of a process feeds back as input, you get a loop. **Positive feedback amplifies** (microphone -> speaker -> microphone = screech). **Negative feedback stabilizes** (thermostat detects heat -> turns off heater -> cools -> turns on). Most complex systems are tangles of both.
example: Social media: you post -> get likes -> feel good -> post more -> get more likes. That's positive feedback -- it amplifies. But there's also negative feedback: post too much -> people unfollow -> fewer likes -> you post less.
probe: A bank run is pure positive feedback: one person withdraws -> others panic -> more withdraw -> more panic. What would a negative feedback mechanism look like that could stop it? (This is literally what deposit insurance was designed to be.)

title: Indirection: the power of one more layer
idea: "Any problem in CS can be solved by adding another level of indirection." Instead of pointing to the thing, **point to something that points to the thing**. This lets you change what the pointer targets without changing everything that uses it.
example: Your contact list has phone numbers. But people change numbers. DNS: you type `google.com`, not `142.250.80.46`. The name is indirection -- it can point to different servers without you knowing. Variables in code: the name `x` is indirection over a memory address.
probe: Every URL is indirection (name -> IP). Every variable name is indirection (name -> value). Every API is indirection (interface -> implementation). What's the cost of all this indirection? When does adding another layer make things worse?

title: Invariants: what doesn't change
idea: The deepest patterns aren't about what changes -- they're about **what stays the same while everything else changes**. These are called invariants, and finding them is the master skill in both math and engineering.
example: Shuffle a deck of cards however you like. The number of cards stays at 52. That's an invariant. Now: can you shuffle so that every red card has a black card next to it? *Knowing the invariant (52 cards, 26 red, 26 black) constrains what's possible.*
probe: In a chess game, pieces move, get captured, positions change constantly. But what's an invariant? (Hint: think about the color of the square a bishop sits on.)

title: Logarithms are about orders of magnitude
idea: A logarithm answers: **how many times do I multiply?** That's it. `log10(1000) = 3` because 10 x 10 x 10 = 1000. Logarithms turn multiplication into addition and turn exponential growth into straight lines. That's why they're everywhere.
example:
```
1         -> log10 = 0
10        -> log10 = 1
100       -> log10 = 2
1,000     -> log10 = 3
1,000,000 -> log10 = 6
```
Each step is 10x bigger, but the log only goes up by 1. This is how your ears work -- a sound 10x more powerful sounds only "one step" louder. Decibels are logarithmic.
probe: The Richter scale is logarithmic. A magnitude 7 earthquake is *10 times* more powerful than a 6, and *100 times* more than a 5. Why would scientists choose a scale that hides how dramatic the differences really are?

title: Patterns are compression
idea: A pattern is anything that lets you **predict what comes next** without seeing it. The more accurately you can predict, the deeper the pattern you've found. This is literally what compression algorithms do -- find patterns to make data smaller.
example: The sequence `2, 4, 6, 8, ...` -- you instantly know the next number is 10. Your brain compressed the rule to "add 2." But `2, 4, 6, 8, 11, ...` breaks the pattern. That discomfort you feel? That's your pattern recognizer screaming.
probe: A random sequence like `7, 3, 9, 1, 5, ...` has no pattern -- which means it *can't be compressed*. Does this mean randomness contains the most information? (Surprisingly, yes -- this is a real result in information theory.)

title: Probability: updating beliefs
idea: Probability isn't about coins and dice -- it's a **language for reasoning under uncertainty**. The core move: you have a belief (prior), you see evidence, you update. This is Bayes' theorem, and it's how rational thinking works.
example: You hear hoofbeats. Prior belief: probably horses (you're in Texas). But you're at the zoo. Updated belief: maybe zebras. You see black-and-white stripes through the fence. Updated again: almost certainly zebras. Each piece of evidence shifts your belief.
probe: A medical test is 99% accurate. You test positive for a disease that affects 1 in 10,000 people. What's the chance you actually have it? (It's much lower than 99% -- work through why. This is the most common probability mistake people make.)

title: Recursion: self-reference that bottoms out
idea: Recursion is when something is **defined in terms of a smaller version of itself**, plus a base case that stops it. Without the base case, you get infinite regress. With it, you get an elegant way to solve problems that have nested structure.
example:
```
How to count people in a line:
1. If no one is in front of you: say "1"
2. Otherwise: ask the person ahead
   to count, then add 1
```
Each person solves a smaller problem (shorter line) until someone hits the base case (no one ahead). The answer builds up as the calls return.
probe: Russian nesting dolls are recursive -- each contains a smaller version of itself. File system folders are recursive -- each can contain more folders. What makes some problems *naturally* recursive vs. just loopy?

title: State: the hidden variable
idea: A function with no state always gives the same output for the same input. A function *with* state can give **different outputs depending on what happened before**. Most bugs come from unexpected state. Most complexity comes from managing it.
example: A light switch: same input (flip), different output (on -> off, off -> on). The switch has *state* -- it remembers whether it's on or off. A calculator with a memory button has state. A pure math function like sin(x) does not.
probe: A traffic light cycles green -> yellow -> red -> green. It's stateful -- the next color depends on the current one. But the *sequence* is fixed. Is a traffic light more or less complex than a light switch? What makes state hard to reason about?

title: Stocks and flows
idea: Every system has **stocks** (accumulations -- things you can count at a moment) and **flows** (rates -- things you measure per unit time). Confusion between stocks and flows is the #1 cause of bad reasoning about systems.
example: A bathtub. **Stock:** water in the tub (gallons). **Inflow:** faucet (gallons/minute). **Outflow:** drain (gallons/minute). The water level rises when inflow > outflow. Obvious here -- but people confuse stock and flow constantly with money, population, CO2.
probe: National debt is a *stock*. The deficit is a *flow*. If the deficit drops from $1T to $500B, is the debt going up or down? (Up -- the deficit is still positive, so the stock is still growing, just slower.)

title: Symmetry is a pattern of sameness
idea: Symmetry means: you can **transform something and it looks the same**. This isn't just about mirrors. Rotating a square 90 degrees gives the same shape. Translating a wallpaper pattern gives the same design. Every symmetry is a transformation that preserves structure.
example: The equation `x² + y² = 1` (a circle). Swap x and y -- still true. Negate x -- still true. Rotate by any angle -- still true. The circle has *infinite* symmetry, which is why it shows up everywhere in nature.
probe: A square has exactly 8 symmetries (4 rotations + 4 reflections). A circle has infinite. What shape has exactly 1 symmetry -- only the identity (doing nothing)?
